title: iOS App Leadership That Went Sideways
raw: Freelance iOS app for hyperlocal event discovery and community building at Inika Design Studio. First time leading a team — planning, coordinating with a UI designer, main client contact. Project ran 5+ months late. Team lacked iOS experience. Client postponed the launch.
refined: My first experience leading a development team taught me more about accountability than any successful project could. When delays pushed our delivery back by months on a freelance iOS app, I had to step up, communicate transparently with the client, and rethink how I plan and execute projects. That experience fundamentally changed how I approach building software — set realistic expectations early, break work into clear accountable milestones, and own outcomes not just tasks.
impact: Project delivered despite 5+ month delay; client relationship maintained

title: MUN — Building Internal Tools for 600+ Participant Conference
raw: Under-Secretary General at Kumarans MUN, a 600+ participant Model UN conference. Built a Gatsby website integrated with a CMS for live press updates. Built moderator software using Electron — cross-platform, offline-capable with web sync. Trained committee members on procedures and crisis handling.
refined: While organizing a 600+ participant conference as Under-Secretary General, I identified operational bottlenecks and solved them with software — a CMS-driven Gatsby website for live press updates and cross-platform Electron moderator software used in live sessions. That experience shaped how I think about engineering: not as isolated code, but as systems that support real people under real constraints. Training users turned out to matter as much as building the tool.
impact: 600+ participant conference, tools used live during sessions

title: Drone + CNN Research — Near Real-Time Crime Detection
raw: Research project at PES University — real-time crime detection using drone footage and CNNs. Published paper. Worked on latency, data pipeline, and real-world deployment constraints.
refined: Working on near real-time crime detection using drone imagery exposed me to the gap between building models and deploying them in real environments. I had to think beyond accuracy metrics and focus on latency, reliability, and system integration. Models are only useful within system constraints — data quality and pipeline design matter as much as architecture. Published a paper on the findings.
impact: Published paper, real-world deployment constraints addressed
relevance_signals: applied AI,real-world ML,latency,data pipeline,systems thinking,beyond accuracy,research

title: CTF and Ethical Hacking Workshop
raw: Attended a hands-on ethical hacking workshop. Solved a vulnerable box, participated in practical exploitation exercises. Learned to think about systems from the attacker's perspective.
refined: My exposure to security through CTF-style challenges taught me to think about systems from both sides — not just how they're built, but how they fail. Systems fail in unexpected ways, and security is often about overlooked assumptions. That adversarial mindset continues to influence how I design and debug software today. Debugging and hacking share the same core skill: reasoning about what the system actually does versus what you assume it does.
impact: Solved vulnerable box; changed approach to system design

title: 8 Years of Sunday Volunteering — Ramakrishna Math
raw: Every Sunday from grade 4 through grade 12, woke at 6 AM, took two buses to reach Ramakrishna Math by 7 AM, helped serve food to thousands of people, stayed to clean up. Later mentored younger participants in the program.
refined: For eight years, my Sundays started before sunrise — two buses across the city to volunteer by 7 AM, serving and cleaning up meals for thousands. No external reward, no audience. Later I mentored younger participants. That experience instilled a sense of discipline and humility that continues to shape how I approach teamwork and responsibility. Showing up consistently, doing unglamorous work without complaint, and understanding that leadership is service rather than authority.
impact: 8 years, thousands of people served weekly, later mentored younger participants

title: Cabbie — Multi-Agent Ride-Sharing via GUI Traversal
raw: Built Cabbie — a multi-agent system where a platform orchestrator dispatches parallel agents, each running in a separate Android emulator navigating a different ride-sharing app (Uber, Lyft, Curb). Agents traverse the UI, extract prices, and book the cheapest option. No APIs — pure GUI interaction using Claude Agent SDK and Node.js orchestration. Called it a post-API paradigm.
refined: I built Cabbie — a multi-agent system that books the cheapest ride by dispatching parallel agents into Android emulators running Uber, Lyft, and Curb simultaneously. No APIs: each agent navigates the UI as a human would, extracts prices, and the orchestrator picks the winner. I used the Claude Agent SDK for individual agents and Node.js for orchestration. The core insight — a post-API paradigm — is that many real-world applications have no public API or restrict them heavily, but an agent that interacts through the GUI bypasses all of that. This project shaped how I think about agentic systems: memory, context, and tool use matter more than raw model capability.
impact: Working demo — parallel agents across 3 ride-sharing apps, no APIs required
relevance_signals: agentic systems,multi-agent,tool use,orchestration,real-world AI,post-API,Claude Agent SDK,parallel execution
